elements.sent-2411-159: In this section, we report results from the principal component analysis (PCA) that transforms the multi-dimensional correlated variables into a smaller set of the principal components (PCs, directions) computed from the linear combinations capturing the largest variation in the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-160: PCs are eigenvectors that represent the weight of eigenvalues of the covariance matrix with the largest explaining the direction of the most variability explained by the given component.
elements.sent-2411-161: The first PC captures the most variability.
elements.sent-2411-162: The eigenvector times the square root of the eigenvalue gives the component loadings which can be interpreted as the correlation of each item with the PC.
elements.sent-2411-163: In order to decide on the number of components required to explain the proportion of the total variance explained by each PC, we calculate the cumulative explained variance ratio - the ratio of its eigenvalue to the sum of all the eigenvalues of all PCs measuring the relative contribution of each PC to explain the amount of variance.
elements.sent-2411-164: In our context, we aggregate an average of \(94.76\) percent cumulative EVR explained by \(10\) PCs.
elements.sent-2411-165: Figure~\ref{fig-corrleationPlot-withPCA is representative subplots of bivariate relationships between the \(10\) PCs from a pool of one hundred experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-166: Each sub-plot shows correlation between defined by the direction of maximum variance impacted by the linear reduction of multi-dimensional data into uncorrelated features ordered by the values of the explained variance of each component.
elements.sent-2411-167: Directions of PCs are not uniquely determined by the component itself, therefore, reading it from either axis does not alter the meaning of the plot itself.

elements.sent-2411-290: We proposed two variations of RF to detect unlawful insider trading and elicit relevant features based on interpretable statistical scores from detailed trades and financial data.
elements.sent-2411-291: With balanced accuracy and sensitivity in mind, we evaluated the performance of each model in hyperparameter-tuned settings.
elements.sent-2411-292: In addition, we replicated steps in the equivalent method proposed by DCZ for meaningful comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-293: We extended the DCZ method by semi-manually labeling data, randomly selecting transactions from multiple industries, removing undefined qualitative features, and fed into an automated pipeline to train and test the fitted model.
elements.sent-2411-294: Our results demonstrate that the model successfully uncovered latent structures in the data, gained better performance than DCZ for each method and boosted the interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-295: We can confirm that governance, financial and trading features may be relevant and only a few selected variables may naturally contribute to uncovering fraudulent behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-296: Our assessment avoided optimistical biased performance with the introduction of additional features indicating that the model can concretely learn and distinguish relevant unlawful transactions.
elements.sent-2411-297: This was achieved by implementing a range of hyperparameters in 5-fold cross-validation by minimizing generalization error.
elements.sent-2411-298: Therefore, we extend the results obtained by the DCZ study with the following highlights:.
elements.sent-2411-299: Low Fall Out Rate: In detecting unlawful transactions, it is very important that the proposed method minimizes mistakes to identify unlawful transactions.
elements.sent-2411-300: As an analogous to a situation when a person guilty of a crime is acquitted, comparing FPR with benchmark methods in Table~\ref{tbl-rfComparativeConfusionMatrixBenchMarkMethod , the proposed method commensurate proportionate or better performance.}
elements.sent-2411-301: The model successfully minimizes false alarms by not wrongfully classifying unlawful as lawful.
elements.sent-2411-302: Because FPR metrics are not controlled by researchers it reduces arbitrary judgment and solves the limitations of traditional econometric studies associated with a priori settings of arbitrary significance levels.
elements.sent-2411-303: Such finding is important to the SEC to confide ML methods to relieve labor intense activities to redirect to enhance rule-making to tracking of the uncaptured unlawful insider trading transactions.
elements.sent-2411-304: Low Miss Rates: By having lower miss rates, the model makes very few mistakes to classify lawful as unlawful transactions.
elements.sent-2411-305: Analogous to a situation when a non-criminal is incarcerated, if a lawful transaction is investigated as unlawful, the burden and stakes are high.
elements.sent-2411-306: Our proposed method reports it misses only a few instances and wrongfully classifies lawful as unlawful.
elements.sent-2411-307: However, our method is substantially better to flag false negatives.
elements.sent-2411-308: High Sensitivity: The proposed method reliably detected the presence of lawful transactions which translates into that wrongful classification of unlawful is low or vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-309: This is ``strong evidence'' of higher true positives and low false negatives.
elements.sent-2411-310: As a starting point, the enforcement agencies can build robust systems and refine methods to further improve the performances thus they can focus on detailed ramifications of unlawful activities.
elements.sent-2411-311: High Specificity: The model correctly identified unlawful as unlawful which excludes the lawful transactions resulting higher number of true unlawful (true negatives) and a low number of unlawful transactions that are wrongly classified as lawful.
elements.sent-2411-312: High Accuracy: In each scenario, the effectiveness as measured by the accuracy of classification, the proposed method correctly identifies lawful and unlawful transactions better than each of the benchmark methods.
elements.sent-2411-313: We demonstrated that a larger pool of data and unboxing time-window archives better accuracy which is pragmatic for the SEC to experiment in the production level data.
